text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Angela Carini of Italy left with a broken nose and covered in blood after 46 seconds of boxing against Algeria's Imane Khelif, who was previously disqualified for failing a gender eligibility test

PARIS — Angela Carini stood before the world’s media a broken fighter, her nose shattered by a punch thrown by Imane Khelif, an opponent who a year ago was disqualified from the world championships for failing a gender eligibility test. Despite that, theInternational Olympic Committee (IOC)deemed Algeria’s Khelif a legitimate opponent.

Carini took only two punches in the fight, both stiff right hands, in the first round of an Olympic Games that she was contesting, she disclosed, in honour of her father. The first broke the chin strap of her head guard, the second landed flush on her nose. Her Olympics lasted 46 seconds and the outcome left her bereft. She cried uncontrollably in the mixed zone and said she had never been hit so hard.

Her blue tunic bore its own testimony, coloured red about the waistband where the blood from her nose had collected.

If the IOC would not accept the verdict of the International Boxing Association (IBA), who say last year’s testing on Khelif and Taiwan’s Lin Yu-ting found X-Y (male) chromosomes instead of X-X (female), and higher levels of testosterone consistent with male biology, perhaps they will take note of Carini’s devastating account of a fight against a DSD (differences of sexual development) athlete that was over before it started.

“I couldn’t carry on with the match. I have a big pain on my nose. I said ‘stop’. It’s better to avoid [more harm] than to keep going. My nose started dripping from the first hit. You need maturity even in this, and it’s fine like this. I wanted to go for my last kilometre for my father but I couldn’t make it.

“I said to myself, for the experience I have and the maturity as a woman that I’ve got, I told myself, to my country and my dad, not to be mad at me, but I stopped [fighting]. I said ‘stop’ for myself. It could be the match of my life, but in that moment I had to safeguard my life too. I felt [I had] to do this. I didn’t have any fear. I don’t have any fear of the ring or of getting hit.”

She pointed out that she frequently trains with her brother “but I felt too much pain today.” It was an extraordinary episode in a story that is becoming increasingly toxic.

It was unclear in the confusion whether the conclusion to the bout was a protest or a genuine stoppage. There was too little usable information, save for the distress she appeared to be suffering. In the end it was neither. Carini simply could not go on, and abandoned.

She went to her corner, tore off her head guard and threw it at her second. She then appeared to finger her nose to check its form. Her corner examined her but there was no question of her continuing.

The fight had been smashed out of her. Clearly distraught, she was led away from her media conference by a member of the Italian delegation, who had flagged her concern to a colleague via a sequence of increasingly urgent gestures.

Defeat is a cold, cold place even intemperatures approaching 30C. Her torso heaved as she retreated towards the changing room with her head buried in her hands, the only witnesses to her torment a gnarly bunch of reporters stunned by what they had seen and heard.

The circumstances of the defeat were among the worst possible outcomes for the IOC, who at best look complacent in their management of the gender issue and at worst negligent.

The other fighter at the centre of the row, Lin, begins her campaign on Friday against Sitora Turdibekova of Uzbekistan. Were Turdibekova to suffer similar harm, or worse, the call for appropriate action, at least some kind of new testing, would be deafening.

According to Italian sources, Carini came under considerable pressure from the boxing community to forfeit the match in protest at the presence in the draw of Khelif and Lin.

That was never an option for her, but it might be for others in a story that is gathering considerable pace with this result. “I am sorry not to have taken my Italy on the podium,” she said, adding: “I wish her to carry on until the end and that she can be happy. I am someone who doesn’t judge anyone. I am not here to give judgements.”

The IOC used the same unspecified criteria to assess eligibility as it did in Tokyo in 2021, which both fighters disqualified by the IBA passed. In a statement they made clear the pair complied “with the competition’s eligibility and entry regulations, as well as all applicable medical regulations”.

What those regulations might be the IOC has not made clear, so we are left with the results of the IBA, an organisation that was banned by the IOC in June last year over governance and corruption issues.

Ordinarily testing at the Olympics is the responsibility of the respective sporting federations, but in the absence of a legitimate alternative the IOC is the de facto regulator, as it was in Tokyo.

The issue with that is the lack of clarity over the degree to which boxers are tested at all, save for producing a passport stamped female. Algeria’s Olympic Committee (CAO) have condemned the treatment of Khelif as unfair. She said her exclusion at the world championships in Delhi was political, to deny Algeria victory. Though she initially challenged that judgment at the Court of Arbitration, she later withdrew her appeal.

And now this, two sledgehammer right hands that for the doubters were as persuasive as any test in the debate about Khelif’s right to participate.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: Two punches that sparked the biggest controversy of the Paris Olympics
author: Kevin Garside
description: Angela Carini of Italy left with a broken nose and covered in blood after 46 seconds of boxing against Algeria's Imane Khelif, who was previously disqualified for failing a gender eligibility test
